RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 15: Vanilla AJAX (XMLHttpRequest)
Day 15 of my reincarnated life. This chapter is all about native AJAX.
AJAX stands for "Asynchronous JavaScript and XML." Back in the early days of web development, most data got sent to the server through a

submission. The problem? After submitting a form, the page usually re-requested the whole screen. And since user experience keeps getting more important, AJAX became something you can't ignore. With the old approach, once you submitted a form you also had to wait for the server to send back a brand new page — so you were waiting on two things, and you'd often stare at a blank screen in the meantime.

So why use AJAX? The nice thing is we just send data to the server through JavaScript, and the current page stays exactly where it is. No need to re-request the whole screen from the backend, which means no blank page flash. Better for the user, and lighter on the server too.
So the biggest win with AJAX is this:
you can fetch new data and update the screen without refreshing the user's current page
, which makes for a much smoother experience.
AJAX doesn't need any plugins either — it just needs the user to allow JavaScript to run. So if you turn JavaScript off, AJAX won't work.
For a more detailed intro to AJAX, I'd recommend the

So that's the low-level AJAX tech. But this is the old way of doing things — it's not really suited for modern web development anymore, and one wrong move can drop you straight into Callback Hell (I'll cover that later). Personally, when I write AJAX I reach for a third-party library like Axios or jQuery's AJAX API. And if I don't want to use a library, I go with the Fetch API that HTML5 added later. It looks like this:
// once you've got the data, pass it to the next then
// catch runs when an error happens
You can see it's a lot cleaner than the original XHR approach, and it lets you dodge Callback Hell. But there's one thing to watch out for with the Fetch API: it doesn't support Internet Explorer 11.

Day 15 of my reincarnated JS life. This time we dig into AJAX — what it actually is, why it changed the web, and how the native API XMLHttpRequest works under the hood. We'll also peek at the modern Fetch API and the old jQuery AJAX style so you can compare them side by side.
